title: Vector Similarity Metrics
learning_outcomes: Derive Cosine Similarity and analyze its mathematical bounds.
Contrast Cosine Similarity with Jaccard and Euclidean metrics.
Explain when to use which metric based on data representations.

content_sections.title: Cosine Similarity Mathematics
content_sections.content:
Cosine Similarity measures the cosine of the angle between two multi-dimensional vectors in a vector space:

$$\text{sim}(\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}) = \cos(\theta) = \frac{\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v}}{\|\mathbf{u}\|_2 \|\mathbf{v}\|_2} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^d u_i v_i}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^d u_i^2} \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^d v_i^2}}$$

If the vectors contain only non-negative elements (such as TF-IDF or count frequencies), the similarity score lies in the range $[0, 1]$. Cosine similarity evaluates direction rather than magnitude, rendering it invariant to document length.

content_sections.title: Alternative Metrics: Jaccard and Euclidean
content_sections.content:
Depending on the text representation, other similarity metrics can be used:

1.  **Jaccard Similarity:** Measures overlap between two binary sets (e.g. present/absent vocabulary words). It is defined as the size of the intersection divided by the size of the union:

$$J(A, B) = \frac{|A \cap B|}{|A \cup B|}$$

2.  **Euclidean Distance:** Measures the straight-line distance between two points in a Euclidean space:

$$d(\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}) = \|\mathbf{u} - \mathbf{v}\|_2 = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^d (u_i - v_i)^2}$$

Euclidean distance is sensitive to vector magnitudes, making it poor for raw frequency vectors of varying document lengths unless the vectors are first L2-normalized.

callouts.title: Equivalence of Normalized Euclidean and Cosine
callouts.content:
For two L2-normalized vectors $\mathbf{u}$ and $\mathbf{v}$ (where $\|\mathbf{u}\| = \|\mathbf{v}\| = 1$), the squared Euclidean distance relates directly to Cosine similarity:

$$\|\mathbf{u} - \mathbf{v}\|_2^2 = \|\mathbf{u}\|^2 + \|\mathbf{v}\|^2 - 2(\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v}) = 2 - 2\cos(\theta)$$

Minimizing Euclidean distance between normalized vectors is mathematically equivalent to maximizing Cosine similarity.
